retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: MHC II: Found on antigen-presenting cells that interact with CD4 T cells; responsible for presenting exogenous antigens

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The most common triggers of anaphylaxis include foods, medications, insect stings, and allergen immunotherapies. Any substance that can trigger the degranulation of mast cells or basophils can induce anaphylaxis. The definition of allergy and anaphylaxis has varied in the past; in 2005, an interprofessional group of clinical experts was formed to improve recognition and designate criteria for the diagnosis of anaphylaxis. The incidence of anaphylaxis varies between 0.5 to 2% in the general population, and the rate has been increasing. The lifetime prevalence is 1.6%. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Antigen: A molecule that is recognized by a specific antibody or T-cell receptor (TCR)

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Major histocompatibility complex (MHC) I: Found on all nucleated cells, play a significant role in determining “self.” Responsible for presenting intracellular antigens to CD8 T cells

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The antibodies generated by this immune response are proteins that have high specificity and affinity for the antigen/ molecule they were generated against. Kohler and Milstein utilized these principles to conceive what came to be called a "hybridoma" (a fusion cell composed of myeloma cells and splenic B lymphocytes, both murine in origin). [1] [2] [4] These hybrid cells allowed Kohler and Milstein to reliably produce a single antibody clone in large volumes and with a pre-selected specificity, which became known as mAbs. The initial biotechnology of primitive mAbs became quickly problematic when the murine origin of such proteins caused them to become immunogenic and non-sustainable for long-term therapy due to the development of human anti-murine antibodies (HAMA). [4] [5] The increased clearance due to HAMA generation was also accompanied by eventual Immunoglobulin E (IgE) development and anaphylactic reactions on subsequent administrations. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Allergy immunotherapy (AIT),  also referred to as allergen desensitization or hypo-sensitization was first introduced by Leonard Noon and John Freeman in 1911, they proposed that people with hay fever were sensitive to grass pollen toxins. [1] Noon is credited for developing a process involved in the extraction of timothy pollen in distilled water and then boiling it to create an extract. [1] This extract was then injected in increasing doses to alleviate symptoms in patients.  This concept is widely in use today with a modified approach. Currently, the term immunotherapy is used to describe all methods to overcome abnormal immune responses with induction of clonal deletion, anergy, immune tolerance, and immune deviation. [2] However, the term desensitization is "a method, to develop a temporary state of tolerance to an agent responsible for an allergic or hypersensitivity reaction." [3] Furthermore, immunotherapy is a disease-modifying treatment and the effects can last longer even after stopping the treatment, which then provides prophylactic effects. [3] [4] [5] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Peyer Patches -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Peyer patches organize in the form of follicles, which mainly consist of B cells. However, the other immune cells therein are T cells, plasma cells, mast cells, eosinophils, macrophages, and basophils. In the Peyer patches, the B lymphocytes reside in the germinal centers of these follicles, where their maturation takes place, and the T lymphocytes are in the parafollicular zone. The Peyer patches at their apices contain M (microfold) cells, enterocytes expressing major histocompatibility complex class II. They endocytose the particulate form of antigens and take them to the antigen-presenting cells (APCs). One of the 4 main functions of APCs is to shuttle the antigens from tissues to sites of lymphocyte priming, one of which is the Peyer patches.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Immunogen: Protein or carbohydrate that is recognized and sufficiently activates an immune response

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Memory B cells: Long-lasting B-cells that are responsive to one particular antigen and become activated with re-exposure to the same antigen

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Adjuvant: Prolongs the presence of antigen in tissue and enhances the immune response to an antigen; used in acquired or artificial immunization (vaccinations)

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: TH2: The response that occurs in the absence of IL-12 and IFN-gamma; promotes systemic antibody driven response; releases IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, IL-10 cytokines

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Dendritic cells (antigen-presenting cells): Facilitate activation of an antigen-specific response by the innate system; present antigens via major histone complexes to activate CD8 and CD4 T cells

retrieved_docs.title: Type III Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Antigen–antibody immune complexes that form in circulation and subsequently deposit in tissues, trigger inflammation and tissue injury, and mediate type III hypersensitivity reactions. A hypersensitivity reaction is an exaggerated or dysregulated immune response to an antigen, leading to tissue injury in the host. These reactions typically occur in individuals previously sensitized to the antigen, implicating memory immune components in the response. [1] [2] According to the Gell and Coombs classification, the 4 classifications of hypersensitivity reactions are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Many delayed hypersensitivity reactions result from drug exposure. In their native state, most drugs are too small to interact with immune receptors strongly enough to stimulate B- and T cells. However, when bound to intracellular proteins such as albumin or integrins, they become large enough to act as haptens. This hapten-carrier complex functions as a new self-antigen and can stimulate an immunological response from both B- and T cells when presented to the HLA complex. Drugs that are not initially reactive but become reactive after being metabolized in the liver, forming a hapten, are called "prohaptens." Alternatively, some drugs can directly and reversibly bind to immune receptors—a mechanism known as "p-i reactions." This interaction stimulates T cells and leads to hypersensitivity reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Evaluation -- Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Criterion 2: Patients develop 2 or more of the following signs or symptoms after allergen exposure—skin or mucosal involvement, respiratory compromise, hypotension, or persistent gastrointestinal symptoms such as abdominal pain or vomiting. Criterion 3: Hypotension occurring within minutes to hours of known allergen exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Food Allergies (Nursing) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: When the history does not reveal the causative food allergen, allergy testing can be performed. For IgE-mediated disorders, skin prick tests (SPTs) provide a rapid means to detect sensitization to a specific food but has advantages and disadvantages as a positive test suggests the possibility of reactivity to a specific food, around 60% of positive tests do not reflect symptomatic food allergy. In contrast, a negative skin-prick establishes the absence of an IgE-mediated reaction. Therefore more definitive tests, such as quantitative IgE tests or food elimination and challenge, are often necessary to establish a diagnosis of food allergy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 74
retrieved_docs.content: as the causative agent of an adverse reaction to hexamine and is contraindicated in individuals with hypersensitivity. Several agents across these classes share a common safety restriction: tavaborole (a heterocyclic bicyclic compound), butenafine, tolnaftát, fenoprofen, methyldopa (a centrally acting anti‑adrenergic drug) and methoxsalen are all contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity reactions. Monobenzone is noted as a polymeric substance, and the chemical descriptor “bicyklické sloučeniny heterocyklické” is specifically mapped to tavaborole. Collectively, these classifications, mechanistic insights and contraindications provide clinicians with an integrated framework for selecting appropriate dermatologic, antifungal and gynecologic therapies while minimizing adverse effects, especially in hypersensitive or pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2837
retrieved_docs.content: Ethanolamin is recognized as an allowed qualifier within the domain of immunology, indicating that it may be used to modify or specify immunological concepts. Conversely, immunology itself is listed as an allowed qualifier for the therapeutic agents kolesevelam, omalizumab, and oxid dusnatý, meaning that the field of immunology can be used to qualify the classification or description of these drugs. These qualifier relationships support precise documentation and coding by linking ethanolamin to immunological contexts and by linking immunology to the specified medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 340
retrieved_docs.content: emphasizing the need for clinicians to recognize and prevent hypersensitivity‑related adverse events across the entire spectrum of listed pharmaceuticals. Overall, the integrated framework unifies diverse drug classes through a structural‑analogue taxonomy, connects them to a diagnostic radiopharmaceutical, and provides explicit hypersensitivity contraindications, thereby supporting consistent clinical documentation, safe prescribing and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Tolnaftate is contraindicated in patients who have the hypersensitivity condition فرط تحسس, meaning it should not be used in individuals with this allergic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1204
retrieved_docs.content: Methyldopa, fenoprofen, butenafine, and tolnaftate are all contraindicated in patients who have hypersensitivity, meaning that each of these agents should be avoided in individuals with an allergic predisposition to prevent adverse immune reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2166
retrieved_docs.content: Butenafine, an antifungal medication, is contraindicated in patients who have فرط تحسس (hypersensitivity), meaning it should not be prescribed to individuals with a known allergic or hypersensitivity reaction to avoid adverse immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2816
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic approach labeled “Allowed qualifier” is indicated for a range of conditions, including acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة), various injury‑ and infection‑related cases (حالات الإصابات والعدوى), chronic fungal infection (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ), and pathological adhesion (الالتصاق). This therapy is therefore applicable across both immunologic, infectious, and tissue‑related disorders, highlighting its broad relevance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1664
retrieved_docs.content: Aurothioglucose and sodium aurothiomalate are both classified within the structural class of allergens, indicating that each of these gold‑containing compounds has the potential to provoke allergic reactions. Recognizing that both agents belong to the allergen class is important for clinicians when evaluating drug safety, monitoring for hypersensitivity, and managing patients who may be exposed to these substances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: In parasitology, the qualifier “Allowed qualifier” is applicable to both قيلة مثانية and قيلة مستقيمية, indicating that these two entities are recognized and can be described using this permitted descriptor. Conversely, the term “Without” is not associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, meaning that no relationship of “associated with” exists between “Without” and these two entities. This delineation clarifies that while the allowed qualifier is relevant to both قيلة مثانية and قيلة مستقيمية within the parasitological context, the concept “Without” does not pertain to them.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 124
retrieved_docs.content: Both butenafine and tolnaftát are contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). Monobenzone and polyestradiol are described as polymeric substances, indicating their relation to polymer chemistry in pharmaceutical contexts. Collectively, these classifications, mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and safety considerations across dermatology, gynecology, and pharmacovigilance inform clinicians about drug families, chemical structures, and patient‑specific contraindications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2920
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a classification that includes several specific agents relevant to immunology. Within this classification, chromonar, ethanolamin, eflornithin, inhibitors of the Na‑K‑Cl symporter, polymyxins, nikotinylalkohol, silymarin, and tamsulosin are all recognized as permissible qualifiers for immunologie. These substances are therefore considered acceptable or applicable modifiers in the context of immunological research, diagnostics, or therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 160
retrieved_docs.content: mechanism when combined with ACE inhibitors. Aliskiren is an antihypertensive agent derived from amidy precursors, is not an azole (inverse isa azoly), and is contraindicated in preschool children and in patients with hypersensitivity; the same hypersensitivity contraindication applies to the non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug fenoprofen and the antifungal agent butenafine. Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a therapeutic class that includes specific antirheumatic agents such as gold preparations, quinolines (antirheumatic drugs) and penicillamine‑related agents. Gold preparations comprise auranofin 3 mg oral capsules, aurothioglucose and aurothiomalate sodium; both aurothioglucose and aurothiomalate sodium belong to the structural class of allergens, linking them to hypersensitivity and to the broader concept of allergies (alergeny). Quinoline antirheumatic drugs are linked to chinoliny, which is mapped from the lipid‑lowering agent pitavastatin and further

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 142
retrieved_docs.content: hydroxide is linked to the concept of astringency (adstringencia). Finally, quercetin is noted for its potential therapeutic effect in managing food allergy, suggesting its use as a supportive treatment for dietary antigen‑induced reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2644
retrieved_docs.content: Allergic reactions represent a broad category of immune‑mediated responses, and chronic allergic rhinitis (الْتِهابُ الأَنْفِ الأَرَجِيّ الدائم) is a specific form of such reactions. Foods (potraviny) are common triggers that can precipitate allergic reactions, and bran, being a type of food, may act as an allergen in susceptible individuals. Recognizing that certain foods can provoke allergic responses, including chronic allergic rhinitis, is important for patient counseling, avoidance strategies, and overall management of allergic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 578
retrieved_docs.content: Allergic reactions are immune‑mediated responses that can be triggered by certain foods, known in Czech as potraviny. Potraviny serve as common causative agents for allergic reactions, meaning that ingestion of specific food items may provoke these hypersensitivity events. Bran is classified as a type of potraviny, and therefore it can also act as a potential trigger for allergic reactions in individuals who are sensitive to its components. Recognizing that foods such as bran may elicit allergic responses is important for accurate diagnosis, patient counseling, and dietary management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.
